synthesis:
The most useful trick I saw this week: an A/B test that can tell you whether “the treatment worked” because your *creative* changed minds or because the *algorithm* quietly changed who even saw it.

If you’ve ever tried to debug a platform experiment and felt like you were measuring a mixture of “targeting effects” and “message effects,” Pal et al. basically hand you a wrench.

---

## When A/B tests lie (and how to make them confess)

Pal et al. introduce a three-arm design for ad-platform experiments where delivery is mediated by an auction/targeting algorithm. The key move is a “metadata-only treatment” arm: the platform’s optimizer is told the ad is treated (via metadata), but the user-facing creative stays the control.

So we get three groups:

- Control: control metadata + control creative  
- Full treatment: treated metadata + treated creative  
- Metadata-only: treated metadata + **control** creative  

With randomization plus an exclusion assumption (“metadata-only doesn’t directly affect users because they don’t see it”), they can identify a population-average **natural indirect effect** (algorithmic reallocation) and **natural direct effect** (creative impact) *without* the usual sequential ignorability headache that kills most mediation in observational settings.

The practical punchline from their Meta field test: at high bids, the algorithmic channel pushed female impression share up about **+2.07 pp**, while the visible creative channel nudged it **down −0.68 pp**. Net effect looks small (+1.39 pp) and non-significant in the pilot—but the decomposition tells you the platform was doing the heavy lifting, and the creative partly counteracted it. A conventional two-arm test would have blurred that into one muddy “meh.”

For QE folks, this is a design pattern: if your system has an optimizer in the loop, consider experimental arms that “tickle the optimizer” separately from what the end user experiences.

---

## Reliability isn’t just about prediction—it’s about *knowing when to trust it*

Three reliability-flavored papers circle the same theme: confidence signals fail exactly where we need them most (near boundaries, under non-identifiability, under domain shift). But each offers a usable fix.

### Random forests: use the tree’s *decision path* as a per-sample reliability cue  
Park et al. propose weighting trees in a Random Forest based on the “flip pattern” of majority-class labels along the root-to-leaf path—an intuitive proxy for “did this tree wander through contradictory regions before landing here?”

They learn weights via cross-validation in a carefully **class-conditioned** way (critical: naïve weighting quietly crushes minority recall due to confounding). The average accuracy gain is small (**~+0.0011**), but statistically consistent across 30 datasets, with essentially no majority-recall regressions. This feels like a safe, incremental bolt-on when you’re already using RFs and care about stability near the decision boundary.

### Model selection under non-identifiability: stop trusting BIC and Laplace when posteriors have ridges  
Foo et al. show why evidence approximations that assume “posterior ≈ ellipse” can badly undercount models with practical non-identifiability (ridge/funnel geometries). Their robust adaptive multiple importance sampling (AMIS) uses Pathfinder-based mixture proposals and then optimizes mixture weights to reduce variance.

In a 64-model ODE simulation, their approach gets model posteriors close to bridge sampling: total variation distance around **0.013** vs **0.11** for standard AMIS, and vastly better than BIC/Laplace. If you do reliability modeling with dynamical systems (degradation, growth/decay, epidemiology-style hazard models), this is a quiet warning: “cheap” evidence can be systematically biased toward identifiable models—not necessarily the right ones.

### Uncertainty under domain shift: ID calibration is a mirage  
Moulaeifard et al. benchmark cuffless BP estimation from PPG across in-distribution and multiple OOD datasets. The headline is familiar but worth repeating: uncertainty quality degrades hard under shift, and you only learn that by testing externally.

Deep ensembles improve OOD accuracy; Gaussian NLL training gives better native uncertainty than MSE; and post-hoc recalibration (conformal prediction, temperature scaling, isotonic) can rescue otherwise-useless MSE uncertainty intervals. Translation: if you’re deploying probabilistic regressors, treat recalibration as part of release, not an academic afterthought.

---

## The thread to pull next week  
We’re watching a broader convergence: **designing experiments and inference pipelines that separate mechanism from measurement**—algorithm vs creative, boundary vs interior, identifiable vs sloppy, ID vs OOD.

Worth pondering: where in your current workflow are you still reporting a single number (lift, accuracy, “model probability”) when the system is really a mixture of channels that deserve to be teased apart?

papers.title: Algorithm or Creative? A Three-Arm Experimental Design for Decomposing Algorithmic Bias in Platform A/B Tests
Decision-Path Patterns as Tree Reliability Signals: Path-based Adaptive Weighting for Random Forest Classification
Reliable model selection in the presence of parameter non-identifiability
papers.authors: Yong See Foo|Torkel E. Loman|Alexander P. Browning|Ivo Siekmann|Ruth E. Baker|Jennifer A. Flegg

papers.title: Uncertainty Reliability Under Domain Shift: An Investigation for Data-Driven Blood Pressure Estimation in Photoplethysmography
papers.authors: Mohammad Moulaeifard|Ciaran Bench|Philip J. Aston|Nils Strodthoff
